spec_match.rationale: Two iframes both load the same `frame.html` and differ only by the `allow="focus-without-user-activation 'none'"` attribute — that is exactly the spec mechanism this feature adds.

interactive.rationale: The iframes are live and the policy attribute is real, but the parent page never reads `document.activeElement` or surfaces a focus indicator — so the user has to infer whether the right frame succeeded by absence of behaviour. The blurb promised `Watch the focus indicator update to see the difference in real time` — there is no such indicator.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature-index blurb says `Watch the focus indicator update in real time`. The page has no focus indicator — only static `badge-allow` / `badge-deny` labels that never change.

edge_cases.rationale: Only the timer-driven `element.focus()` path is exercised. Spec covers `autofocus`, `dialog.showModal()`, `showPopover()`, and `window.focus()` — and explicitly preserves user-initiated focus. None of those branches are demonstrated on this page.

design_system.rationale: Variables-only colours, but the page includes a generic `live-probe` panel whose probe lists `CSS.supports`, `customElements`, `navigator.gpu`, `PerformanceObserver`, `scheduler` — none of which are related to the focus policy under review. That is template-card boilerplate the design rules explicitly forbid.

openQuestions.title: Remove the generic live-probe panel
openQuestions.detail: The `[data-live-probe]` panel script probes `navigator.gpu`, `scheduler`, `customElements` etc — none of which relate to `focus-without-user-activation`. That is exactly the static-template behaviour the design system forbids. Either delete the panel or replace its checks with `document.featurePolicy?.allowsFeature('focus-without-user-activation')` and a per-iframe report.

openQuestions.title: Add a live `document.activeElement` indicator on the parent
openQuestions.detail: The blurb promises a focus indicator. Mount a small fixed-position readout that updates every 250ms with the active element's tag + which iframe owns it. That converts the demo from `the right one didn't move so trust us` into something you can actually see ticking.

openQuestions.title: Exercise autofocus, dialog.showModal, showPopover variants
openQuestions.detail: Spec lists five focus paths it suppresses (`element.focus`, `window.focus`, `autofocus`, `dialog.showModal`, popover `showPopover`). The page text lists them all but only one is wired up. Add a panel with five `try this` buttons inside each iframe so the visitor can see each variant blocked.

openQuestions.title: Show user-initiated focus still works in both frames
openQuestions.detail: The whole point is that user clicks/taps still focus. Add a `click-to-focus` input in both iframes so the visitor can verify the policy doesn't break legitimate UX — currently the page asserts this in prose without demonstrating it.

summary: The headline mechanic (paired iframes, one carrying the new `allow` value) is exactly right and the spec match is good, but the page is undermined by a generic capability-probe panel that has nothing to do with the focus policy and a missing focus indicator that the blurb literally promises. The page also stops at one of the five focus paths the policy covers.
